Answer: In Swift each loop iteration has a fresh `i` binding, so they print 0, 1, 2; but if you captured a single mutable variable declared outside the loop, all closures would share it and print its final value â€” adding `[i]` to snapshot the current value guarantees the per-iteration value regardless.

q: How are tuples represented and used in Swift, and what are their limitations?
a: A tuple is a lightweight, ad-hoc value type that groups several values, optionally with labels, like `(name: String, age: Int)`. They're laid out in memory as their elements packed together, similar to a struct, and they're value types with copy semantics. Tuples are great for transient grouping, multiple return values, and pattern-matching destructuring, but they have real limitations: they can't conform to protocols (with a few special-cased exceptions like `Equatable`/`Comparable`/`Hashable` for small tuples added later), can't have methods or stored computed members, and very large tuples are awkward. For anything that needs identity, methods, or protocol conformance, a struct is the right tool.
d: Memory-wise a tuple is laid out like an anonymous struct: elements in order with alignment/padding, and `MemoryLayout` reports the combined size. Element labels are part of the type for matching purposes but don't change layout. Swift added `Equatable`, `Comparable`, and `Hashable` conformance for tuples of up to six elements where the elements themselves conform (implemented via compiler magic / variadic-generic-like support), so `(1, "a") == (1, "a")` works, but you still can't make a tuple conform to your own protocol or extend tuples with methods. Tuples support destructuring (`let (a, b) = pair`), labeled access (`pair.name`) and positional access (`pair.0`), and they're used pervasively for multiple return values. Single-element labeled tuples are essentially collapsed by the compiler. A subtlety historically caused by tuples: argument-tuple splatting (passing a tuple where multiple parameters are expected) was removed in Swift 3, so you can't directly splat a tuple into a function's parameters anymore. With variadic generics (Swift 5.9+), tuples of parameter packs enable more generic operations over heterogeneous element lists. Tuples are also used in pattern matching for `switch` over multiple values simultaneously.
f: Trap: Can you make a tuple type conform to your own custom protocol via an extension? Answer: No â€” Swift does not allow extending tuple types or declaring conformances on them; only the standard library's special-cased `Equatable`/`Comparable`/`Hashable` (for small arities) exist, so if you need protocol conformance you must wrap the tuple in a struct.

q: What is `@discardableResult` and when should you use it?
a: `@discardableResult` is an attribute you put on a function or method to suppress the compiler warning that fires when its return value is ignored at the call site. By default Swift warns when you call a non-`Void` function and don't use its result, since that often indicates a bug. You apply `@discardableResult` when the return value is genuinely optional for the caller â€” for example a builder method that returns `self` for chaining, or an operation whose result (like a removed element or a success flag) is sometimes irrelevant. It's purely a diagnostic annotation and changes no runtime behavior.
d: The unused-result warning exists because ignoring a return value frequently signals a mistake â€” forgetting to use a computed value, or expecting a mutating method when the function actually returns a new value (a common error with value-type APIs like `sorted()` vs `sort()`). `@discardableResult` tells the compiler 'it's legitimate to ignore this here,' so callers don't need `_ = foo()` to silence the warning. Typical applications: fluent/builder APIs returning `Self`, `@discardableResult func remove(at:) -> Element` where you sometimes just want the side effect, and Combine/async APIs returning cancellables or tasks you may or may not retain. The inverse mechanism is the absence of the attribute combined with the implicit warning, and there's no way to force callers to use a result short of not marking it discardable. A subtlety: marking something `@discardableResult` can hide genuine bugs if overused, so it should be reserved for cases where ignoring the result is a normal, intended usage. It applies per-declaration and is inherited considerations for protocol requirements must be repeated on conformances if desired. It does not affect overload resolution or types.
f: Trap: Does adding `@discardableResult` change the function's type or how it participates in overload resolution? Answer: No â€” it's purely a compile-time diagnostic suppressor with zero effect on the function's type, ABI, runtime behavior, or overload resolution; it only stops the 'result of call is unused' warning at call sites.

q: When should you choose a struct versus a class for a given type? Give a principled framework.
a: My default is a struct, and I switch to a class only when I have a concrete reason. I reach for a class when I need reference identity (two references pointing to the same shared mutable instance), when I need inheritance, when I need `deinit` for cleanup, or when I must interoperate with Objective-C / Cocoa APIs that expect classes. I use a struct for values, data models, and anything where copy semantics and immutability-by-default make reasoning easier â€” which is most types. The guiding principle is value semantics versus reference semantics: do you want independent copies or shared identity?
d: The deeper reasoning is about semantics and safety. Value types eliminate a whole class of bugs from unintended sharing and aliasing â€” when you pass a struct, the callee can't mutate your copy â€” which is why Swift's standard library models `String`, `Array`, `Dictionary`, and numbers as structs (with copy-on-write for efficiency). Classes are necessary when identity is part of the concept (a network connection, a view controller, a cache) or when many parts of the system must observe and mutate one shared object. Performance also factors in: small structs avoid heap allocation and ARC overhead and can live in registers, while classes always allocate and reference-count; but a struct with many reference-type fields still incurs ARC on copy, and a huge struct copied frequently can be slower than sharing a class, so it's not absolute. Inheritance is class-only, but protocols + composition + generics often replace inheritance more cleanly with value types. `deinit` is class-only because value types don't have a single owner whose deallocation is observable. Threading: value types passed across threads are safer because copies don't share state, which aligns with Swift concurrency's `Sendable` model. The principled question chain: need identity? need inheritance? need deinit? need ObjC interop? If none, use a struct.
f: Trap: You make a 'model' a struct for value semantics but it has a stored closure capturing other objects and you mutate it from multiple threads â€” what hidden problem remains? Answer: Value semantics give you independent copies, but if the struct's stored closure or class-typed fields capture/reference shared mutable objects, those are still shared by reference across copies, so you haven't actually isolated the mutable state â€” copying the struct doesn't deep-copy referenced objects, reintroducing data races.

q: Explain how `@objc` and `dynamic` interact with Swift's dispatch, and when each is required.
a: `@objc` exposes a Swift declaration to the Objective-C runtime, generating the selector and metadata needed for ObjC to call it, but on its own it doesn't force Swift to use message dispatch for Swift-to-Swift calls. `dynamic` tells Swift to dispatch the member dynamically through the Objective-C runtime's `objc_msgSend`, which is what enables runtime features like method swizzling and KVO. In practice you write `@objc dynamic` together when you need that fully dynamic, swizzlable behavior â€” for example properties observed by KVO or methods you intend to swizzle. Pure Swift code uses static or vtable dispatch and doesn't need either attribute.
d: There are two orthogonal axes: visibility to the ObjC runtime (`@objc`) and dispatch mechanism (`dynamic`). `@objc` alone makes a member callable from ObjC (via a registered selector) and usable with selector-based APIs like `#selector` and target/action, but Swift callers may still use direct or vtable dispatch. `dynamic` forces every access â€” even from Swift â€” to go through `objc_msgSend`, which is the only dispatch mode that can be intercepted by swizzling at runtime; that's why KVO requires `@objc dynamic` properties (KVO works by dynamically subclassing and overriding the setter via the runtime). `@objc` is implied in some contexts (members of `NSObject` subclasses overriding ObjC methods, `@IBAction`/`@IBOutlet`), but `dynamic` must be explicit. The cost is performance: `objc_msgSend` dispatch is slower than direct/vtable and prevents inlining and most optimizations. A common misconception is that `@objc` alone enables swizzling â€” it doesn't; you need `dynamic` for the call to actually route through the swizzlable runtime path. Note `dynamic` requires `@objc` (on Apple platforms) because it relies on the ObjC runtime; there's no pure-Swift dynamic dispatch override mechanism for this. Also, `final` and `dynamic` are mutually exclusive in intent â€” `final` removes overridability, `dynamic` maximizes runtime malleability.
f: Trap: You add `@objc` to a property and try to observe it with KVO but observations never fire â€” why? Answer: `@objc` alone keeps Swift dispatch (direct/vtable), so the setter isn't routed through `objc_msgSend` and KVO's dynamically-injected override never runs; you must mark it `@objc dynamic` so the access goes through the ObjC runtime where KVO can intercept the setter.

q: Explain how copy-on-write works for Swift's value types like `Array`, and how you'd implement it for your own type.
a: Copy-on-write, or COW, is how Swift gives types like `Array`, `String`, and `Dictionary` value semantics without copying their backing storage on every assignment. These types are structs that wrap a reference to a heap-allocated buffer class; copying the struct just copies the pointer and bumps the buffer's reference count, so multiple values can share one buffer. The actual element copy is deferred until someone mutates a value while the buffer is shared â€” at that point the mutating code checks whether the buffer is uniquely referenced and, if not, makes a private copy first. You implement it yourself by wrapping a class buffer in a struct and calling `isKnownUniquelyReferenced` before mutating.
d: The mechanism hinges on `isKnownUniquelyReferenced(&storage)`, a standard-library function that returns true only when the strong reference count of the class instance is exactly one (and it must be a native Swift class, not `@objc`). In a custom COW struct, each mutating method first calls this; if the buffer is shared (count > 1), it allocates a fresh copy of the buffer and reassigns the struct's reference to it before mutating, ensuring no other value observes the change â€” preserving value semantics. If the buffer is uniquely owned, it mutates in place with zero copying, which is the fast path. This is why appending to an `Array` you hold the only reference to is O(1) amortized, but appending after you've handed a copy elsewhere triggers a full O(n) buffer copy on the first mutation. A subtlety: `isKnownUniquelyReferenced` can report false even when logically unique if a temporary extra reference exists (e.g., captured by an in-flight closure), and it doesn't work with weak/unowned or bridged ObjC objects. Exclusivity enforcement and COW interact: an `inout` access establishes exclusive access, which helps the optimizer prove uniqueness. The common misconception is that assigning an array always copies it â€” it doesn't; only mutation-while-shared does.
f: Trap: Why does `isKnownUniquelyReferenced` sometimes return `false` for a buffer you believe is solely owned, and what's a requirement for it to work? Answer: It requires a native (non-`@objc`) Swift class and counts the actual strong references at that instant â€” a transient extra reference (a captured copy, an in-flight access, or bridging) inflates the count, so it conservatively returns `false`, correctly triggering a defensive copy rather than risking shared mutation.
